article_title: Webb Reveals a Sulfur-Shrouded Super-Earth With a Permanent Magma Ocean, Defining a New Class of Exoplanet

content_preview.title: Webb Reveals a Sulfur-Shrouded Super-Earth With a Permanent Magma Ocean, Defining a New Class of Exoplanet
content_preview.summary: JWST observations of super-Earth L 98-59 d reveal a sulfur-dominated atmosphere sustained by a perpetual magma ocean, a combination that fits no existing planetary category and may define a new class of exoplanet.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A rocky planet slightly larger than Earth, orbiting a red dwarf star just 35 light-years away, has upended the way astronomers classify small worlds. Observations from the James Webb Space Telescope show that L 98-59 d possesses a thick atmosphere dominated by hydrogen sulfide and sulfur dioxide, sustained by a global ocean of molten rock that has persisted for billions of years. The findings, [published on March 16 in Nature Astronomy](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41550-026-028...

editor_notes.source_verification: ScienceDaily confirms all key claims: L 98-59 d at 35 light-years, 1.6x Earth size, sulfur-dominated atmosphere, magma ocean thousands of km deep, Harrison Nicholls at Oxford, Nicholls quote verified verbatim, Nature Astronomy publication March 16, collaborators from Groningen/Leeds/ETH Zurich. Nature paper exists at cited DOI (paywall redirect).
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All key facts verified: planet dimensions, discovery date (2019), atmospheric composition (H2S and SO2), magma ocean mechanism, photochemistry producing SO2. The two-category classification framework (gas dwarfs vs water worlds) is accurately presented as the context being challenged.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Excellent science journalism. Technical concepts explained clearly without condescension. The 'rotten eggs' description adds accessibility without undermining seriousness.
editor_notes.originality: Well-structured coverage of a genuinely novel scientific finding. The explanation of how the magma ocean sustains the atmosphere is particularly well done.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Strong space science news at 650 words. Well-sourced from Nature Astronomy paper and ScienceDaily. The new exoplanet classification angle is compelling and accurately presented.
